Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 â†’ 4.6, 4.6 â†’ 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- cma-doctor: Validate Concept Model Kit installation
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- cma-normalize: Apply whitespace normalization to concept model documents
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- cma-validate: Validate concept model links and annotations
- cma-render: Transform concept model to ClaudeMark format
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Next: capture-declared-pasteboard-flavors (â‚¢A9AAY) [rough]

Docket:
  ## Character
  
  Mechanical capture work on top of one genuine unknown (cocoa/objc2 FFI shape for NSPasteboard enumeration). First tranche is a small spike â€” figure out the right call pattern for `NSPasteboard.generalPasteboard()`, item enumeration, declared-types list, and per-type `dataForType:` bytes. The rest is plumbing that matches the classification-tag pattern already laid down in `apcrh_harvest.rs`. The detector is **not touched** â€” it still reads UTF-8 via arboard. This pace is strictly "put every declared flavor on disk so the evaluation pace has real inputs."
  
  ## Problem
  
  Ann's empirical `osascript -e 'clipboard info'` showed five declared UTIs after an Epic Copy All: `public.rtf` (139559 bytes), `public.utf16-plain-text` (52834), `public.utf8-plain-text` (26461 â€” exactly what the classifier saw), `Unicode text` (52832, legacy UTF-16 alias), and a 52-byte `string`. **No `public.html`** â€” contradicting the â‚¢A9AAW docket's premise. Word pastes formatted because it reads `public.rtf`.
  
  Current harvest, layered on arboard, sees at most two flavors: `get_text()` (UTF-8 plain) and `get().html()` (which honestly returns `ContentNotAvailable` against a board that has no HTML). Arboard has no public API for RTF, nor for enumerating declared UTIs. To get the full picture on disk we have to go one layer lower.
  
  Without this pace, the evaluation pace that follows has nothing to evaluate against â€” only the UTF-8 flavor that already failed once.
  
  ## Solution
  
  Use cocoa/objc2 FFI directly against `NSPasteboard` to (a) enumerate the **declared** types of the first pasteboard item and (b) fetch each type's bytes via `dataForType:`. Write each flavor to the journal directory alongside the existing capture pattern, preserving the Clinical/NonClinical tag from `apcrh_Classification`. Only *declared* types are captured â€” not macOS-synthesized ones (those can balloon to 15+ per item; what the producer actually wrote is what we want to study). This matches the behavior of the `osascript 'clipboard info'` diagnostic.
  
  Filename shape: `{N}-in.{tag}.{ext}` where `{ext}` is derived from the UTI via a small mapping table. Unknown UTIs fall through to a sanitized `.{com-example-thing}.bin` form so the flavor space is extensible without code changes.
  
  ## Deliverables â€” Code
  
  - **New module** `apcrk_pasteboard.rs` (prefix `apcrk` â€” K for kernel/pasteboard, following the source-module convention). Single public entry: `apcrk_capture_declared_flavors(dir, classification, index) -> Result<Vec<String>, String>` returning the list of filenames written. Internals: `unsafe` cocoa/objc2 calls for `generalPasteboard`, `pasteboardItems`, `types`, `dataForType`. FFI surface kept tight and type-safe at the boundary.
  - **UTI-to-extension mapper** â€” data-driven table. Known mappings:
    - `public.rtf` â†’ `rtf`
    - `public.utf8-plain-text` â†’ `utf8.txt`
    - `public.utf16-plain-text` â†’ `utf16.txt`
    - `public.html` â†’ `html` (defensive â€” if it ever appears)
    - `public.tiff`, `public.png` â†’ corresponding extensions
    - legacy NSPasteboard names (`NSStringPboardType`, `Unicode text`) â†’ sanitized passthrough
    - anything else â†’ sanitized-uti with `.bin` suffix (dots â†’ hyphens, forbidden filename chars scrubbed)
  - **`apcrh_harvest::apcrh_capture_all_flavors`**: delegate to the new module. The existing hand-coded arboard `get_text` / `get().html()` writers become a fallback *only* if the new path returns zero flavors (defensive â€” should never happen in practice).
  - **`apcap_main::zapcap_handle_focus`**: no changes beyond the existing harvest call site. Classifier still reads UTF-8 via arboard's `get_text()`.
  - **Cargo.toml**: add `objc2` and any `objc2-app-kit` / `objc2-foundation` subcrates needed. Pin versions.
  - **Tests** (`apctk_pasteboard.rs`):
    - UTI-to-extension mapping, known and unknown UTIs.
    - Sanitization of forbidden filename characters in unknown UTIs.
    - FFI paths cannot be unit-tested without a real NSPasteboard; verification is via deployed run on Ann's machine.
  
  ## Deliverables â€” Spec (APCPS)
  
  - **Clipboard Harvest â†’ Flavors table**: replace the current 2-row (`text`/`html`) table with a description of declared-flavor enumeration. Note that the table becomes illustrative â€” actual flavors captured depend on what the producer (Epic, browser, any app) wrote.
  - **Clipboard Harvest â†’ Naming paragraph**: extend extension description to include UTI-derived mapping and the `.{sanitized-uti}.bin` fallback.
  - **Clipboard Harvest â†’ Scope note**: explicit statement that only declared types are captured, not macOS-synthesized ones. Reference the `osascript 'clipboard info'` diagnostic as the parity target.
  - **Short note in the Flavors section**: arboard remains on the classifier's `get_text()` path; the new FFI module owns enumeration + multi-flavor capture. Separation of concerns is load-bearing â€” arboard is simple and stable, the FFI module is lower-level and riskier.
  
  ## Constraints
  
  - **Declared types only** â€” never capture macOS-synthesized derivatives. Match `osascript 'clipboard info'` output.
  - **arboard stays on the classifier path.** No regression to `get_text()`-based detection. The FFI module writes to disk; the classifier reads through arboard.
  - **FFI failures are non-fatal.** A broken NSPasteboard call logs via `apcrl_error_now!` and lets harvest fall back to the arboard path. Triage continues regardless.
  - **Tag classification preserved across all flavors** â€” `10001-in.clinical.rtf`, `10001-in.clinical.utf8.txt`, etc. The enum decided at the start of the focus handler applies uniformly.
  - **Index space unified.** Scanner in `apcrh_harvest` keeps the leading-digit-run parse â€” every new extension counts toward the same index space.
  - **Unsafe is isolated.** All FFI lives in `apcrk_pasteboard.rs`. No `unsafe` bleeds into `apcrh_harvest` or `apcap_main`.
  
  ## Exit Criteria
  
  - `tt/apcw-t.Test.sh` passes, including new UTI mapping tests. `deny(warnings)` holds.
  - Focus on Epic "Copy All" produces on disk: `{N}-in.clinical.rtf`, `{N}-in.clinical.utf16.txt`, `{N}-in.clinical.utf8.txt`, and any other declared UTIs present. **RTF must be present with nonzero size** â€” primary acceptance signal.
  - Focus on a plain-text non-clinical clipboard still produces at least `{N}-in.nonclinical.utf8.txt`.
  - Unknown UTIs (if Epic or some other source writes them) land as `.{sanitized}.bin` rather than failing the capture.
  - APCPS Clipboard Harvest section updated: Flavors table reshaped, Naming paragraph extended, scope note on declared-only added.
  - Deployed to Ann's machine; next Copy All verified to produce RTF + UTF-16 + UTF-8 files in `~/apcjd/`.
  
  ## Out of Scope
  
  - **Interpreting any captured flavor.** Reading RTF, converting RTFâ†’HTML, enriching text parsing â€” all belong to the evaluation pace (â‚¢A9AAX) and beyond.
  - **Changing the classifier's entry point** from `get_text()` to anything richer. That's a downstream implementation pace.
  - **Selecting a permanent RTF parser** as a Cargo dep. The evaluation pace decides; this pace does not.
  - **Synthesized UTIs.** Only declared ones.
  - **Image flavor interpretation.** `.tiff` / `.png` bytes land on disk; they are not decoded, thumbnailed, or forwarded.
  - **Cross-platform parity.** This is Mac-first; Windows pasteboard enumeration is a future pace.

Docket:
  ## Character
  
  Mechanical capture work on top of one genuine unknown (cocoa/objc2 FFI shape for NSPasteboard enumeration). First tranche is a small spike â€” figure out the right call pattern for `NSPasteboard.generalPasteboard()`, item enumeration, declared-types list, and per-type `dataForType:` bytes. The rest is plumbing that matches the classification-tag pattern already laid down in `apcrh_harvest.rs`. The detector is **not touched** â€” it still reads UTF-8 via arboard. This pace is strictly "put every declared flavor on disk so the evaluation pace has real inputs."
  
  ## Problem
  
  Ann's empirical `osascript -e 'clipboard info'` showed five declared UTIs after an Epic Copy All: `public.rtf` (139559 bytes), `public.utf16-plain-text` (52834), `public.utf8-plain-text` (26461 â€” exactly what the classifier saw), `Unicode text` (52832, legacy UTF-16 alias), and a 52-byte `string`. **No `public.html`** â€” contradicting the â‚¢A9AAW docket's premise. Word pastes formatted because it reads `public.rtf`.
  
  Current harvest, layered on arboard, sees at most two flavors: `get_text()` (UTF-8 plain) and `get().html()` (which honestly returns `ContentNotAvailable` against a board that has no HTML). Arboard has no public API for RTF, nor for enumerating declared UTIs. To get the full picture on disk we have to go one layer lower.
  
  Without this pace, the evaluation pace that follows has nothing to evaluate against â€” only the UTF-8 flavor that already failed once.
  
  ## Solution
  
  Use cocoa/objc2 FFI directly against `NSPasteboard` to (a) enumerate the **declared** types of the first pasteboard item and (b) fetch each type's bytes via `dataForType:`. Write each flavor to the journal directory alongside the existing capture pattern, preserving the Clinical/NonClinical tag from `apcrh_Classification`. Only *declared* types are captured â€” not macOS-synthesized ones (those can balloon to 15+ per item; what the producer actually wrote is what we want to study). This matches the behavior of the `osascript 'clipboard info'` diagnostic.
  
  Filename shape: `{N}-in.{tag}.{ext}` where `{ext}` is derived from the UTI via a small mapping table. Unknown UTIs fall through to a sanitized `.{com-example-thing}.bin` form so the flavor space is extensible without code changes.
  
  ## Deliverables â€” Code
  
  - **New module** `apcrk_pasteboard.rs` (prefix `apcrk` â€” K for kernel/pasteboard, following the source-module convention). Single public entry: `apcrk_capture_declared_flavors(dir, classification, index) -> Result<Vec<String>, String>` returning the list of filenames written. Internals: `unsafe` cocoa/objc2 calls for `generalPasteboard`, `pasteboardItems`, `types`, `dataForType`. FFI surface kept tight and type-safe at the boundary.
  - **UTI-to-extension mapper** â€” data-driven table. Known mappings:
    - `public.rtf` â†’ `rtf`
    - `public.utf8-plain-text` â†’ `utf8.txt`
    - `public.utf16-plain-text` â†’ `utf16.txt`
    - `public.html` â†’ `html` (defensive â€” if it ever appears)
    - `public.tiff`, `public.png` â†’ corresponding extensions
    - legacy NSPasteboard names (`NSStringPboardType`, `Unicode text`) â†’ sanitized passthrough
    - anything else â†’ sanitized-uti with `.bin` suffix (dots â†’ hyphens, forbidden filename chars scrubbed)
  - **`apcrh_harvest::apcrh_capture_all_flavors`**: delegate to the new module. The existing hand-coded arboard `get_text` / `get().html()` writers become a fallback *only* if the new path returns zero flavors (defensive â€” should never happen in practice).
  - **`apcap_main::zapcap_handle_focus`**: no changes beyond the existing harvest call site. Classifier still reads UTF-8 via arboard's `get_text()`.
  - **Cargo.toml**: add `objc2` and any `objc2-app-kit` / `objc2-foundation` subcrates needed. Pin versions.
  - **Tests** (`apctk_pasteboard.rs`):
    - UTI-to-extension mapping, known and unknown UTIs.
    - Sanitization of forbidden filename characters in unknown UTIs.
    - FFI paths cannot be unit-tested without a real NSPasteboard; verification is via deployed run on Ann's machine.
  
  ## Deliverables â€” Spec (APCPS)
  
  - **Clipboard Harvest â†’ Flavors table**: replace the current 2-row (`text`/`html`) table with a description of declared-flavor enumeration. Note that the table becomes illustrative â€” actual flavors captured depend on what the producer (Epic, browser, any app) wrote.
  - **Clipboard Harvest â†’ Naming paragraph**: extend extension description to include UTI-derived mapping and the `.{sanitized-uti}.bin` fallback.
  - **Clipboard Harvest â†’ Scope note**: explicit statement that only declared types are captured, not macOS-synthesized ones. Reference the `osascript 'clipboard info'` diagnostic as the parity target.
  - **Short note in the Flavors section**: arboard remains on the classifier's `get_text()` path; the new FFI module owns enumeration + multi-flavor capture. Separation of concerns is load-bearing â€” arboard is simple and stable, the FFI module is lower-level and riskier.
  
  ## Constraints
  
  - **Declared types only** â€” never capture macOS-synthesized derivatives. Match `osascript 'clipboard info'` output.
  - **arboard stays on the classifier path.** No regression to `get_text()`-based detection. The FFI module writes to disk; the classifier reads through arboard.
  - **FFI failures are non-fatal.** A broken NSPasteboard call logs via `apcrl_error_now!` and lets harvest fall back to the arboard path. Triage continues regardless.
  - **Tag classification preserved across all flavors** â€” `10001-in.clinical.rtf`, `10001-in.clinical.utf8.txt`, etc. The enum decided at the start of the focus handler applies uniformly.
  - **Index space unified.** Scanner in `apcrh_harvest` keeps the leading-digit-run parse â€” every new extension counts toward the same index space.
  - **Unsafe is isolated.** All FFI lives in `apcrk_pasteboard.rs`. No `unsafe` bleeds into `apcrh_harvest` or `apcap_main`.
  
  ## Exit Criteria
  
  - `tt/apcw-t.Test.sh` passes, including new UTI mapping tests. `deny(warnings)` holds.
  - Focus on Epic "Copy All" produces on disk: `{N}-in.clinical.rtf`, `{N}-in.clinical.utf16.txt`, `{N}-in.clinical.utf8.txt`, and any other declared UTIs present. **RTF must be present with nonzero size** â€” primary acceptance signal.
  - Focus on a plain-text non-clinical clipboard still produces at least `{N}-in.nonclinical.utf8.txt`.
  - Unknown UTIs (if Epic or some other source writes them) land as `.{sanitized}.bin` rather than failing the capture.
  - APCPS Clipboard Harvest section updated: Flavors table reshaped, Naming paragraph extended, scope note on declared-only added.
  - Deployed to Ann's machine; next Copy All verified to produce RTF + UTF-16 + UTF-8 files in `~/apcjd/`.
  
  ## Out of Scope
  
  - **Interpreting any captured flavor.** Reading RTF, converting RTFâ†’HTML, enriching text parsing â€” all belong to the evaluation pace (â‚¢A9AAX) and beyond.
  - **Changing the classifier's entry point** from `get_text()` to anything richer. That's a downstream implementation pace.
  - **Selecting a permanent RTF parser** as a Cargo dep. The evaluation pace decides; this pace does not.
  - **Synthesized UTIs.** Only declared ones.
  - **Image flavor interpretation.** `.tiff` / `.png` bytes land on disk; they are not decoded, thumbnailed, or forwarded.
  - **Cross-platform parity.** This is Mac-first; Windows pasteboard enumeration is a future pace.

67	  - `public.rtf` â†’ `rtf`
68	  - `public.utf8-plain-text` â†’ `utf8.txt`

70	  - `public.html` â†’ `html` (defensive â€” if it ever appears)

content.content: 1 # Ann's PHI Clipbuddy â€” Prototype Specification 2 3 ## Scope 4 5 This document specifies the engineering pipeline for the proof-of-life prototype. It covers detection algorithms, tech stack, data sources, project structure, and scope boundaries. It does NOT cover the user experience â€” see [APCAS-Specification.md](APCAS-Specification.md) for the UX plan. 6 7 This is a living document. The pipeline will evolve rapidly during prototype development. Changes here do not require parallel updates to the product spec. 8 9 ## Scope Boundaries 10 11 The prototype deliberately excludes: 12 13 - **No JavaScript** â€” All rendering logic is Rust. The Tauri webview is a passive display surface receiving complete HTML from the Rust backend. Inline `onclick` attributes use the Tauri bridge primitive (`window.__TAURI__.core.invoke`) for command dispatch only â€” no `.js` files, no JS application logic, no JS state management. This eliminates the language-boundary bug class (e.g., byte-offset vs char-offset mismatch at the Rust-JS serialization boundary). 14 - **No persistence** â€” no logs, no audit trail, no history of anonymized notes 15 - **One patient at a time** â€” no batch processing, no queue 16 - **No configuration** â€” no custom blacklists, no user preferences, no settings UI 17 - **No click-to-highlight** â€” clicking a finding does not scroll/highlight in the document preview (future feature) 18 - **No multi-word merge** â€” adjacent name hits are individually flagged, not merged into a single token 19 20 ## Tech Stack 21 22 | Layer | Crate | Version | License | Rationale | 23 |-------|-------|---------|---------|-----------| 24 | Clipboard | `arboard` | 3.x | MIT/Apache | Only crate with cross-platform HTML clipboard read (`get_html()`) | 25 | HTML parsing | `scraper` | latest | MIT | DOM + CSS selectors, built on Mozilla's html5ever | 26 | Dictionary matching | `aho-corasick` | latest | MIT/Unlicense | Single-pass multi-pattern O(n) regardless of dictionary size | 27 | Pattern matching | `regex` | latest | MIT/Apache | Structural PHI patterns | 28 | Word boundaries | `unicode-segmentation` | latest | MIT/Apache | UAX#29 compliant word boundary detection | 29 | GUI shell | `tauri` | 2.x | MIT | Rust-rendered HTML/CSS frontend, system webview as passive display | 30 | File watching | `notify` | latest | MIT/Apache | FSEvents on macOS, ReadDirectoryChanges on Windows | 31 32 All crates are pure Rust, fully Cargo-lockable on macOS and Windows. Tauri requires the system webview (WebKit on macOS, WebView2 on Windows â€” both ship with the OS). The webview receives pre-rendered HTML from Rust on every state change; no JavaScript application code exists in the project. 33 34 ### Tauri CLI cwd Constraint 35 36 The `cargo tauri build` command discovers its project by walking up from the current working directory looking for `Cargo.toml` and `tauri.conf.json`. It has no `--manifest-path` equivalent. Additionally, `tauri.conf.json` uses relative paths (e.g., `"frontendDist": "./ui"`) that resolve from the Tauri project directory. 37 38 This means `cargo tauri build` must run with cwd set to `Tools/apck/apcd/`. This is an external tool constraint, not a design choice. All other cargo commands (`cargo run`, `cargo test`) use `--manifest-path` and run from the project root per BCG convention. The two `cargo tauri` call sites (`apcc_build`, `apcc_deploy`) use BCG isolation subshells to contain the `cd`. 39 40 ## Input: Epic "Copy All" HTML 41 42 Epic's "Copy All" places HTML on the clipboard with structural formatting: 43 44 - `Label: value` patterns for demographics and metadata 45 - Section headers for clinical categories (Chief Complaint, HPI, PMH, Medications, Vitals, Labs, Assessment/Plan) 46 - Narrative freetext in the History of Present Illness and other sections 47 - Signature blocks with provider names and timestamps 48 49 The HTML structure is semi-structured â€” not arbitrary prose and not fully machine-readable. This structure is the primary advantage over plain text processing: labels anchor PHI detection with high precision. 50 51 ## Clipboard Handling 52 53 ### Clinical Content Heuristic 54 55 Before processing, the engine checks whether clipboard content appears to be clinical. The check scans for known Epic/clinical label patterns: 56 57 **Positive indicators** (presence of 2+ triggers acceptance): 58 - `Patient:`, `DOB:`, `MRN:`, `Attending:`, `Facility:` 59 - `Chief Complaint:`, `History of Present Illness:`, `Assessment/Plan:` 60 - `Vitals:`, `Labs:`, `Medications:`, `Allergies:` 61 - HTML `Label:` structure characteristic of Epic formatting 62 63 If clinical content is detected, the engine proceeds to the detection pipeline and clears the system clipboard (writes empty string via `arboard::set_text("")`). 64 65 If clinical content is NOT detected, the app clears any previous triage state and returns to the initial instruction view. The app displays a brief diagnostic line showing: clipboard content length, content type (HTML/plain text), and the first ~100 characters of content. This diagnostic aids debugging when real Epic data fails the heuristic â€” the developer can see what was on the clipboard and update the label list accordingly. 66 67 This heuristic will be refined when tested against real Epic output. The initial label list is calibrated to pass the synthetic test fixture. 68 69 ### Clipboard Change Detection 70 71 On window focus, the engine compares the current clipboard content (byte-for-byte string comparison) against the last successfully consumed content. If identical, the existing triage state is preserved â€” no reprocessing. If different, the clinical content heuristic runs on the new content. Clinical notes are a few KB; storing the last consumed string for comparison has negligible memory cost at prototype scale. 72 73 ## Journal Directory 74 75 **Purpose.** One place on disk that accumulates runtime artifacts produced by the app: verbatim clipboard harvests from every focus with changed content (clinical and non-clinical alike), and a running log of every `apcrl_*` emission teed from stdout. A single location keeps the mental model honest â€” one `ls` answers both "what did the app capture?" and "what did the app report?". 76 77 **Path.** `$HOME/apcjd/` â€” outside any repo tree. The directory is created lazily at application startup (for the log tee) or on first capture (for harvests), whichever comes first. A `.gitignore` entry for `apcjd/` is present as belt-and-suspenders against a misdirected `$HOME` resolution that lands in-tree. 78 79 **Contents.** Two independent features share this directory: 80 81 | Artifact | Producer | Shape | 82 |----------|----------|-------| 83 | Harvest captures | `apcrh_harvest` on every focus with changed content | `{N}-in.{tag}.{ext}` where `{tag}` is `clinical` or `nonclinical` (see Clipboard Harvest) | 84 | Anonymized outputs | `apcap_main` focus handler on Clinical branch | `{N}-out.txt` (see Clipboard Harvest) | 85 | Observability log | `apcrl_log` file-tee sink | `apcap.log` (see Observability Log) | 86 87 ## Clipboard Harvest 88 89 **Purpose.** Preserve verbatim real-world clipboard contents from every focus with changed content so the fixture library can grow from real traffic, so Epic's actual flavor structure can be studied, and so a classifier misfire still leaves an artifact on disk to post-mortem. The prototype currently ships with synthetic fixtures only; harvest is the bridge from lab to field without relying on the clinician to remember to save. 90 91 **Trigger.** Every focus with changed clipboard content â€” executed after the classifier runs so the verdict can be tagged into the filename, but before any Clinical-branch clipboard zero-out. The same-as-last short-circuit at the top of the focus handler still applies: re-focusing with unchanged content does not re-harvest. 92 93 **Storage.** The journal directory (`$HOME/apcjd/`, see above). 94 95 **Naming.** Inputs are written as `{N}-in.{tag}.{ext}` where `{tag}` is `clinical` or `nonclinical` â€” the classifier's verdict at the moment of capture. Clinical captures additionally pair with an anonymized output written as `{N}-out.txt`. `N` seeds at 10000 for an empty directory, otherwise `max_leading_digit_run + 1` across every filename whose name begins with digits. Files sharing the same `N` group all artifacts of one capture â€” e.g., `10000-in.clinical.txt`, `10000-in.clinical.html`, and `10000-out.txt` are three artifacts of clinical capture 10000; `10001-in.nonclinical.txt` is the lone artifact of non-clinical capture 10001. Gaps in the numeric sequence are not filled â€” the scan advances past the current maximum. Filenames that do not begin with a digit are ignored in the max calculation, so `apcap.log` and any user-placed `README` or `notes` co-exist without perturbing indexing. The scan parses the leading digit run, not the full stem, so legacy bare `{N}.{ext}` and prior untagged `{N}-in.{ext}` files from earlier prototype runs still count toward index advancement â€” all styles share one index space. 96 97 **Flavors.** Every flavor the `arboard` abstraction exposes at the time of capture: 98 99 | Flavor | Filename | Present when | 100 |--------|----------|--------------| 101 | Plain text (`get_text`) | `{N}-in.{tag}.txt` | Always â€” required for any focus handler entry | 102 | HTML (`get().html()`) | `{N}-in.{tag}.html` | Epic "Copy All" places HTML; absence is possible and non-fatal | 103 104 RTF and image flavors are documented gaps: `arboard` 3.x does not surface RTF, and image capture requires the `image-data` feature which this project does not enable. Dropping to platform-specific pasteboard APIs to close these gaps is explicitly out of scope for the prototype. 105 106 **Anonymized output.** On every Clinical-branch focus, after the input harvest succeeds, the focus handler also writes `{N}-out.txt`: the anonymizer's output with default-elide toggle states (every finding elided). The point is a legible input/output pairing on disk â€” Brad can diff `{N}-in.clinical.txt` against `{N}-out.txt` to review what clipbuddy produced for Ann, without relying on her to paste-and-save. This output is captured *at focus time*, not on copy. Updating `{N}-out.txt` as Ann toggles findings or re-copies is deferred UX; the current snapshot is always the default-elide baseline. Write failure is non-fatal â€” triage continues, and the failure is logged via `apcrl_error_now!`. An HTML-preserving anonymized output (`{N}-out.html`) is out of scope â€” the current anonymizer is plain-text only. Non-clinical captures do not pair with an anonymized output; there is nothing to anonymize because no findings are produced. 107 108 **Failure mode.** A capture failure logs via `apcrl_error_now!` (stdout + tee) and does not abort triage. User-visible behavior is unchanged whether harvest succeeds or fails â€” the triage pipeline is authoritative. The same holds for the anonymized-output write. System-clipboard zero-out happens only on the Clinical branch; a non-clinical capture leaves Ann's clipboard untouched. 109 110 **Privacy posture.** PHI-at-rest stays outside the repo. The journal directory is PHI-permissive by design â€” harvests, the anonymized-output pairings, and the observability log all share the same on-device-only posture: nothing in `$HOME/apcjd/` is auto-committed, auto-uploaded, or auto-anonymized. Review and promotion of any artifact to an out-of-site context is a manual act. 111 112 The always-harvest posture is a deliberate scope expansion over earlier prototype behavior, which only captured on the Clinical branch. Today's compact is "clipbuddy captures every clipboard it sees during a session" â€” not only clinical notes. When Brad reviews a session's journal directory he will observe Ann's non-clinical clipboard activity too: passwords she copied, personal email snippets, URLs, anything at all that hit her pasteboard while Ann's PHI Clipbuddy had focus in the background. This is not a new *kind* of risk â€” the journal directory was already PHI-permissive on-device, and nothing leaves the device automatically â€” but the *scope* of what a reviewer sees is broader, and naming that here means the posture is not discovered by surprise. A debug-mode or opt-out flag for non-clinical capture is deliberately not provided at prototype scale; revisit if Ann or Brad flags the scope as too broad in practice. 113 114 ## Observability Log 115 116 **Purpose.** Give the clinician-developer and the engineer a shared, persistent view of what the app did across runs. The `.app` bundle on macOS captures stdout where a non-technical user won't look for it; a file in the journal directory is where both humans already know to peek. 117 118 **Mechanism.** `apcrl_log` exposes an optional file-tee sink: `apcrl_tee_init(path)` installs a once-only append-open handle. Every subsequent `apcrl_*_now!`, `_if!`, and comparison-variant emission is written twice â€” once to stdout (verbatim) and once to the tee file (same format, same line). One format, two sinks. 119 120 **Format.** Exactly what stdout receives: `[LEVEL] [YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS.mmm] [file:line] message`. The timestamp is local wall-clock with millisecond precision, captured at emit time via `chrono::Local::now()`. Single emission path means no format drift between modalities â€” an engineer debugging a remote bundle and an engineer running `cargo run` read the same lines. The clock lets Ann's "it stopped working around 2pm" kind of report meet the log without re-running under a stopwatch. 121 122 **Location.** `$HOME/apcjd/apcap.log`. Filename matches the binary name (`apcap`) as an engineer mnemonic. Append-only â€” no rotation at prototype scale; a clinical session produces tens of lines, a year of use produces thousands. 123 124 **Init.** At `main()` startup, before any other log emission. Failure to install the tee (missing HOME, disk full, permissions) logs to stdout and proceeds â€” the app starts and stdout remains authoritative. 125 126 **Failure mode.** Tee write failures are silently swallowed inside the emitter to avoid recursive-logging hazards (a failing log should not generate more log traffic). Stdout is the authoritative sink; tee is best-effort. 127 128 **Privacy posture.** The log lives alongside harvests in the journal directory and inherits the same posture â€” the whole directory is PHI-permissive by design. The log may carry clipboard content, PHI-adjacent details, or anything else useful to diagnose behavior on the clinician's machine. The discipline that matters is not "keep the log PHI-free" but "keep the journal directory on the doctor's computer" â€” nothing in `$HOME/apcjd/` is auto-uploaded, auto-committed, or transmitted off-device. Review and promotion of any artifact (capture or log line) to an out-of-site context is a manual act. 129 130 ## Detection Pipeline 131 132 ### Overview 133 134 Three tiers execute in sequence. Results merge by highest severity â€” if a token is flagged by multiple tiers, the most severe classification wins. 135 136 ``` 137 Input: Epic HTML clipboard content 138 | 139 Parse HTML â†’ typed spans with labels and positions 140 | 141 Tier 1: Regex scan â†’ RED 142 | 143 Tier 2: Label-anchored extraction â†’ RED 144 | 145 Tier 3: Dictionary blacklist/whitelist scan â†’ YELLOW or PASS 146 | 147 Merge by highest severity 148 | 149 Output: classified token stream â†’ frontend 150 ``` 151 152 ### Tier 1 â€” Regex Patterns (â†’ RED) 153 154 Structural patterns with low false positive rate. No database dependency. 155 156 **Known ambiguities:** The zip code regex (`\d{5}`) can match 5-digit lab values, identifiers, or dosage amounts. The date regex can match version numbers or other formatted numerics. The street address regex can match non-address text with number-word-suffix patterns. These are accepted risks for the prototype â€” the conservative bias (false positive > false negative) means these appear as RED items the clinician can review. Context-aware refinement is deferred. 157 158 | Pattern | Category | Regex (representative) | Notes | 159 |---------|----------|----------------------|-------| 160 | SSN | `[SSN]` | `\d{3}-\d{2}-\d{4}` | | 161 | Phone | `[PHONE]` | `\d{3}[-.)]\s?\d{3}[-.)]\s?\d{4}`, `\(\d{3}\)\s?\d{3}-\d{4}` | Multiple formats | 162 | Email | `[EMAIL]` | `[\w.+-]+@[\w-]+\.[\w.-]+` | | 163 | Dates | `[DATE]` | `\d{1,2}/\d{1,2}/\d{2,4}`, `\d{1,2}-\d{1,2}-\d{2,4}`, month name patterns | Multiple formats | 164 | Street address | `[ADDRESS]` | `\d+\s+[A-Z]\w+\s+(St\|Dr\|Ave\|Blvd\|Rd\|Ln\|Way\|Ct\|Pl\|Cir\|Drive\|Street\|Avenue\|Boulevard\|Road\|Lane\|Court\|Place\|Circle)` | Followed by optional city/state/zip | 165 | Zip code | `[ADDRESS]` | `\b\d{5}(-\d{4})?\b` | Within address context | 166 | MRN (labeled) | `[MRN]` | Value following `MRN:` label | | 167 | Account (labeled) | `[ACCOUNT]` | Value following `Account` or `Acct` label | | 168 | Encounter ID (labeled) | `[ENCOUNTER_ID]` | Value following `Encounter ID:` label | | 169 170 Dates receive special handling: when a date follows a `DOB:` label, the engine computes age from the current date and emits `Age: NN` instead of `[DATE]` in the anonymized output. Per HIPAA Safe Harbor (45 CFR 164.514(b)(2)(i)(C)), ages over 89 are aggregated: computed age >89 emits `Age: 90+`. 171 172 ### Tier 2 â€” Label-Anchored Extraction (â†’ RED) 173 174 Words immediately following known Epic labels are PHI regardless of dictionary membership. The label is authoritative â€” if Epic says `Patient: Xylophone McZephyr`, that's a name even if no database contains it. 175 176 | Label Pattern | Category | Extraction Rule | 177 |--------------|----------|-----------------| 178 | `Patient:` | `[NAME]` | All text until next label or line break | 179 | `Attending:` | `[PROVIDER]` | All text until next label or line break | 180 | `Provider:` | `[PROVIDER]` | All text until next label or line break | 181 | `Referring:` | `[PROVIDER]` | All text until next label or line break | 182 | `Facility:` | `[FACILITY]` | All text until next label or line break | 183 | `Electronically signed by:` | `[PROVIDER]` | All text until next label or line break | 184 | `Emergency contact` | `[NAME]` | Name portion, phone extracted by Tier 1 | 185 | `primary care physician` | `[PROVIDER]` | Following name (e.g., "Dr. Susan Chen") | 186 187 Label matching is case-insensitive on the label text. Value extraction extends to the next structural boundary (label, line break, or HTML element boundary). 188 189 **Per-word anchoring:** Each word in the extracted value is individually flagged as RED. "Margaret J. Thornton" after `Patient:` produces three separate **anchored** items, each classified `[NAME]`. In anonymized output: `[NAME] [NAME] [NAME]`. No multi-word merge is attempted. 190 191 Items caught by Tier 2 are **anchored** â€” their RED classification is final. They are not subsequently dictionary-checked. If a word is both anchored (Tier 2) and matched (Tier 3), the anchored classification takes precedence. 192 193 ### Vocabulary: Anchored vs. Matched 194 195 Two independent mechanisms flag words as potential PHI. To avoid confusion, the spec distinguishes: 196 197 - **Anchored**: Flagged by Tier 2 because of *position* â€” the word follows a known label. Always RED. Independent of any dictionary. 198 - **Matched**: Flagged by Tier 3 because of *identity* â€” the word appears in a name/location dictionary. YELLOW (unless also anchored, in which case RED wins). 199 200 A word can be both anchored and matched (e.g., "Thornton" after `Patient:` AND in the Census surname list). Anchored always takes precedence. The term "blacklist" in this spec refers specifically to the Tier 3 name/location dictionaries; Tier 2 label-anchored extraction is a separate mechanism. 201 202 ### Tier 3 â€” Dictionary Blacklist/Whitelist (â†’ YELLOW or PASS) 203 204 Catches names and locations in narrative text that lack label anchors (e.g., "her husband Robert Thornton called 911"). 205 206 **Case handling:** All dictionary lookups are case-insensitive. The aho-corasick automaton is built from lowercase dictionary entries. Input tokens are lowercased before matching. Original case is preserved in display and anonymized output. 207 208 **Process:** 209 1. Segment text into words using `unicode-segmentation` (UAX#29 boundaries) 210 2. Lowercase each token for lookup; preserve original for display 211 3. Run aho-corasick automaton against lowercased tokens (single pass, O(n)) 212 4. For each hit, check whitelist membership (also lowercase) 213 5. Classify: 214 - Blacklist hit, no whitelist hit â†’ **YELLOW** (matched, default ELIDE) 215 - Blacklist hit AND whitelist hit â†’ **YELLOW** (collision, default ELIDE) 216 - Whitelist hit only â†’ **PASS** 217 - Neither list â†’ **PASS** 218 219 **Collision resolution context signals** (applied within YELLOW to aid clinician review): 220 - Capitalization mid-sentence suggests a proper noun (name) rather than common word 221 - Adjacent blacklist hits suggest a full name ("Robert Thornton" â€” two hits together) 222 - Section context: names in demographics header vs. narrative HPI carry different weight 223 224 These signals inform the findings panel display but do not change the YELLOW classification in the prototype. The clinician makes the final call. 225 226 ## Dictionary Data 227 228 ### Proof-of-Life Sizing 229 230 Curated-small dictionaries that prove the architecture without requiring full datasets: 231 232 | Dictionary | Source | License | Size | File | 233 |------------|--------|---------|------|------| 234 | Surnames | US Census 2010, top 1,000 by frequency | Public domain | ~1,000 entries | `dictionaries/surnames.txt` | 235 | First names | SSA baby names, top 1,000 | Public domain | ~1,000 entries | `dictionaries/firstnames.txt` | 236 | Cities | US state capitals + top 100 cities by population | Public domain | ~150 entries | `dictionaries/cities.txt` | 237 | Medical whitelist | Hand-curated: common medications, lab tests, diagnoses, anatomy, abbreviations | Original curation | ~500 entries | `dictionaries/medical_whitelist.txt` | 238 | English whitelist | Top 5,000 common English words | Public domain | ~5,000 entries | `dictionaries/english_whitelist.txt` | 239 240 Total: ~8,000 entries, ~80KB on disk. 241 242 ### Full Dataset Sizing (Future) 243 244 | Dictionary | Source | License | Size | 245 |------------|--------|---------|------| 246 | Surnames | US Census 2010, complete | Public domain | ~160,000 | 247 | First names | SSA baby names, complete | Public domain | ~100,000 | 248 | Cities | US Census incorporated places | Public domain | ~30,000 | 249 | Medical whitelist | SNOMED-CT + RxNorm + LOINC subsets | NLM license (redistribution constraints) | ~100,000+ | 250 | English whitelist | Full dictionary | Public domain | ~170,000 | 251 252 ## Anonymization 253 254 ### Placeholder Strategy 255 256 Category-specific placeholders preserve semantic context for Open Evidence: 257 258 | PHI Category | Placeholder | Example | 259 |-------------|-------------|---------| 260 | Patient/person names | `[NAME]` | "Ms. [NAME] is a 74-year-old female" | 261 | Dates (non-DOB) | `[DATE]` | "Visit Date: [DATE]" | 262 | Date of birth | `Age: NN` or `Age: 90+` | "DOB: 03/15/1952" â†’ "Age: 74"; ages >89 â†’ "Age: 90+" | 263 | Medical record number | `[MRN]` | "MRN: [MRN]" | 264 | Phone numbers | `[PHONE]` | "phone: [PHONE]" | 265 | Addresses | `[ADDRESS]` | "at [ADDRESS]" | 266 | Email addresses | `[EMAIL]` | | 267 | Social Security numbers | `[SSN]` | | 268 | Facility names | `[FACILITY]` | "Seen at [FACILITY]" | 269 | Provider names | `[PROVIDER]` | "Dr. [PROVIDER]" | 270 | Account numbers | `[ACCOUNT]` | | 271 | Encounter IDs | `[ENCOUNTER_ID]` | | 272 273 ### Output Format 274 275 Plain text with preserved section structure (line breaks, section headers). HTML formatting is stripped â€” this is itself a privacy measure, removing embedded metadata and hidden fields. The output should be readable when pasted into Open Evidence's chat input. 276 277 ## Self-Update Mechanism 278 279 The application watches `/Users/Shared/apcua/` for new `.app` bundles using the `notify` crate (FSEvents backend on macOS). On detection: 280 281 1. Copy new bundle over current application path 282 2. Spawn `open -n ` to launch new version 283 3. `std::process::exit(0)` to terminate current process 284 285 No confirmation dialog. The watcher runs on a background thread independent of the main Tauri event loop. If the watch directory does not exist at startup, the app creates it silently. 286 287 ## Project Structure 288 289 ``` 290 Tools/apck/ 291 apck-claude-context.md # Build/run/deploy instructions (RCG reference) 292 APCAS-Specification.md # Application spec (UX vision) 293 APCPS-PrototypeSpecification.md # This document (pipeline engineering) 294 apcw_workbench.sh # Bash workbench 295 apcz_zipper.sh # Zipper enrollment 296 test_fixtures/ 297 epic_progress_note.html # Synthetic Epic clipboard data 298 apcd/ # Rust/Tauri source directory 299 Cargo.toml # Two [[bin]]: apcap (app), apcal (fixture loader) 300 src/ 301 apcap_main.rs # Tauri app entry point 302 apcal_main.rs # Fixture loader entry point 303 lib.rs # Engine library + test module declarations 304 apcre_engine.rs # PHI detection orchestrator 305 apcte_engine.rs # Tests for engine 306 apcrp_parse.rs # HTML clipboard parsing 307 apctp_parse.rs # Tests for parser 308 apcrm_match.rs # Dictionary/regex matching 309 apctm_match.rs # Tests for matching 310 apcrd_dictionaries.rs # Dictionary loading 311 apctd_dictionaries.rs # Tests for dictionaries 312 apcru_update.rs # Directory watcher + self-update (no unit tests) 313 ui/ 314 index.html 315 style.css 316 dictionaries/ 317 surnames.txt 318 firstnames.txt 319 cities.txt 320 medical_whitelist.txt 321 english_whitelist.txt 322 ``` 323 324 ## Prefix Tree 325 326 ``` 327 apc (non-terminal) 328 â”œâ”€â”€ apca (non-terminal) 329 â”‚ â”œâ”€â”€ apcab â€” App Batch binary (assay â€” detection pipeline on HTML files) 330 â”‚ â”œâ”€â”€ apcad â€” App Dictionary binary (refresh from public sources) 331 â”‚ â”œâ”€â”€ apcal â€” App Loader binary (fixture clipboard tool) 332 â”‚ â”œâ”€â”€ apcap â€” App Prototype binary (Tauri main) 333 â”‚ â””â”€â”€ apcas â€” application specification document (UX, workflow) 334 â”œâ”€â”€ apcc â€” CLI command implementations 335 â”œâ”€â”€ apcd â€” Rust/Tauri source directory 336 â”‚ â”œâ”€â”€ apcrh â€” Clipboard harvest module (writes to journal directory) 337 â”‚ â”œâ”€â”€ apcrj â€” Journal directory path resolver 338 â”‚ â””â”€â”€ apcrl â€” Logging macros (info, error, fatal with file/line) + file-tee sink 339 â”œâ”€â”€ apcj (non-terminal â€” journal) 340 â”‚ â””â”€â”€ apcjd â€” journal directory ($HOME/apcjd/) holding harvests + apcap.log 341 â”œâ”€â”€ apck â€” kit directory 342 â”œâ”€â”€ apcps â€” prototype specification document 343 â”œâ”€â”€ apcs (non-terminal) 344 â”‚ â””â”€â”€ apcs0 â€” detection pipeline specification (MCM concept model) 345 â”œâ”€â”€ apcu (non-terminal) 346 â”‚ â””â”€â”€ apcua â€” update staging directory (/Users/Shared/apcua/) 347 â”œâ”€â”€ apcw â€” workbench 348 â””â”€â”€ apcz â€” zipper 349 ``` 350 351 Rust source file prefixes follow RCG: `{cipher}r{classifier}_{name}.rs` where cipher is `apc`. Test files: `{cipher}t{classifier}_{name}.rs` â€” classifier matches between source and test. See RCG (Rust Coding Guide) for full naming conventions, including `z`-prefix internal declarations, string boundary discipline, and constant discipline. 352 353 `lib.rs` wiring: 354 ```rust 355 pub mod apcre_engine; 356 pub mod apcrp_parse; 357 pub mod apcrm_match; 358 pub mod apcrd_dictionaries; 359 pub mod apcru_update; 360 pub mod apcrh_harvest; 361 pub mod apcrj_journal; 362 363 #[cfg(test)] mod apcte_engine; 364 #[cfg(test)] mod apctp_parse; 365 #[cfg(test)] mod apctm_match; 366 #[cfg(test)] mod apctd_dictionaries; 367 #[cfg(test)] mod apcth_harvest; 368 ``` 369 370 ## Tabtargets 371 372 | Tabtarget | Colophon | Purpose | 373 |-----------|----------|---------| 374 | `tt/apcw-b.Build.sh` | `apcw-b` | `cargo tauri build` (release) | 375 | `tt/apcw-r.Run.sh` | `apcw-r` | `cargo tauri dev` (local development) | 376 | `tt/apcw-D.Deploy.sh` | `apcw-D` | Build + scp to `anns-macbook-air:/Users/Shared/apcua/` | 377 | `tt/apcw-fl.FixtureLoad.sh` | `apcw-fl` | Run `apcal` to load fixture HTML onto clipboard | 378 | `tt/apcw-t.Test.sh` | `apcw-t` | `cargo test` in `apcd/` | 379

file.content: # Ann's PHI Clipbuddy â€” Prototype Specification ## Scope This document specifies the engineering pipeline for the proof-of-life prototype. It covers detection algorithms, tech stack, data sources, project structure, and scope boundaries. It does NOT cover the user experience â€” see [APCAS-Specification.md](APCAS-Specification.md) for the UX plan. This is a living document. The pipeline will evolve rapidly during prototype development. Changes here do not require parallel updates to the product spec. ## Scope Boundaries The prototype deliberately excludes: - **No JavaScript** â€” All rendering logic is Rust. The Tauri webview is a passive display surface receiving complete HTML from the Rust backend. Inline `onclick` attributes use the Tauri bridge primitive (`window.__TAURI__.core.invoke`) for command dispatch only â€” no `.js` files, no JS application logic, no JS state management. This eliminates the language-boundary bug class (e.g., byte-offset vs char-offset mismatch at the Rust-JS serialization boundary). - **No persistence** â€” no logs, no audit trail, no history of anonymized notes - **One patient at a time** â€” no batch processing, no queue - **No configuration** â€” no custom blacklists, no user preferences, no settings UI - **No click-to-highlight** â€” clicking a finding does not scroll/highlight in the document preview (future feature) - **No multi-word merge** â€” adjacent name hits are individually flagged, not merged into a single token ## Tech Stack | Layer | Crate | Version | License | Rationale | |-------|-------|---------|---------|-----------| | Clipboard | `arboard` | 3.x | MIT/Apache | Only crate with cross-platform HTML clipboard read (`get_html()`) | | HTML parsing | `scraper` | latest | MIT | DOM + CSS selectors, built on Mozilla's html5ever | | Dictionary matching | `aho-corasick` | latest | MIT/Unlicense | Single-pass multi-pattern O(n) regardless of dictionary size | | Pattern matching | `regex` | latest | MIT/Apache | Structural PHI patterns | | Word boundaries | `unicode-segmentation` | latest | MIT/Apache | UAX#29 compliant word boundary detection | | GUI shell | `tauri` | 2.x | MIT | Rust-rendered HTML/CSS frontend, system webview as passive display | | File watching | `notify` | latest | MIT/Apache | FSEvents on macOS, ReadDirectoryChanges on Windows | All crates are pure Rust, fully Cargo-lockable on macOS and Windows. Tauri requires the system webview (WebKit on macOS, WebView2 on Windows â€” both ship with the OS). The webview receives pre-rendered HTML from Rust on every state change; no JavaScript application code exists in the project. ### Tauri CLI cwd Constraint The `cargo tauri build` command discovers its project by walking up from the current working directory looking for `Cargo.toml` and `tauri.conf.json`. It has no `--manifest-path` equivalent. Additionally, `tauri.conf.json` uses relative paths (e.g., `"frontendDist": "./ui"`) that resolve from the Tauri project directory. This means `cargo tauri build` must run with cwd set to `Tools/apck/apcd/`. This is an external tool constraint, not a design choice. All other cargo commands (`cargo run`, `cargo test`) use `--manifest-path` and run from the project root per BCG convention. The two `cargo tauri` call sites (`apcc_build`, `apcc_deploy`) use BCG isolation subshells to contain the `cd`. ## Input: Epic "Copy All" HTML Epic's "Copy All" places HTML on the clipboard with structural formatting: - `Label: value` patterns for demographics and metadata - Section headers for clinical categories (Chief Complaint, HPI, PMH, Medications, Vitals, Labs, Assessment/Plan) - Narrative freetext in the History of Present Illness and other sections - Signature blocks with provider names and timestamps The HTML structure is semi-structured â€” not arbitrary prose and not fully machine-readable. This structure is the primary advantage over plain text processing: labels anchor PHI detection with high precision. ## Clipboard Handling ### Clinical Content Heuristic Before processing, the engine checks whether clipboard content appears to be clinical. The check scans for known Epic/clinical label patterns: **Positive indicators** (presence of 2+ triggers acceptance): - `Patient:`, `DOB:`, `MRN:`, `Attending:`, `Facility:` - `Chief Complaint:`, `History of Present Illness:`, `Assessment/Plan:` - `Vitals:`, `Labs:`, `Medications:`, `Allergies:` - HTML `Label:` structure characteristic of Epic formatting If clinical content is detected, the engine proceeds to the detection pipeline and clears the system clipboard (writes empty string via `arboard::set_text("")`). If clinical content is NOT detected, the app clears any previous triage state and returns to the initial instruction view. The app displays a brief diagnostic line showing: clipboard content length, content type (HTML/plain text), and the first ~100 characters of content. This diagnostic aids debugging when real Epic data fails the heuristic â€” the developer can see what was on the clipboard and update the label list accordingly. This heuristic will be refined when tested against real Epic output. The initial label list is calibrated to pass the synthetic test fixture. ### Clipboard Change Detection On window focus, the engine compares the current clipboard content (byte-for-byte string comparison) against the last successfully consumed content. If identical, the existing triage state is preserved â€” no reprocessing. If different, the clinical content heuristic runs on the new content. Clinical notes are a few KB; storing the last consumed string for comparison has negligible memory cost at prototype scale. ## Journal Directory **Purpose.** One place on disk that accumulates runtime artifacts produced by the app: verbatim clipboard harvests from every focus with changed content (clinical and non-clinical alike), and a running log of every `apcrl_*` emission teed from stdout. A single location keeps the mental model honest â€” one `ls` answers both "what did the app capture?" and "what did the app report?". **Path.** `$HOME/apcjd/` â€” outside any repo tree. The directory is created lazily at application startup (for the log tee) or on first capture (for harvests), whichever comes first. A `.gitignore` entry for `apcjd/` is present as belt-and-suspenders against a misdirected `$HOME` resolution that lands in-tree. **Contents.** Two independent features share this directory: | Artifact | Producer | Shape | |----------|----------|-------| | Harvest captures | `apcrh_harvest` on every focus with changed content | `{N}-in.{tag}.{ext}` where `{tag}` is `clinical` or `nonclinical` (see Clipboard Harvest) | | Anonymized outputs | `apcap_main` focus handler on Clinical branch | `{N}-out.txt` (see Clipboard Harvest) | | Observability log | `apcrl_log` file-tee sink | `apcap.log` (see Observability Log) | ## Clipboard Harvest **Purpose.** Preserve verbatim real-world clipboard contents from every focus with changed content so the fixture library can grow from real traffic, so Epic's actual flavor structure can be studied, and so a classifier misfire still leaves an artifact on disk to post-mortem. The prototype currently ships with synthetic fixtures only; harvest is the bridge from lab to field without relying on the clinician to remember to save. **Trigger.** Every focus with changed clipboard content â€” executed after the classifier runs so the verdict can be tagged into the filename, but before any Clinical-branch clipboard zero-out. The same-as-last short-circuit at the top of the focus handler still applies: re-focusing with unchanged content does not re-harvest. **Storage.** The journal directory (`$HOME/apcjd/`, see above). **Naming.** Inputs are written as `{N}-in.{tag}.{ext}` where `{tag}` is `clinical` or `nonclinical` â€” the classifier's verdict at the moment of capture. Clinical captures additionally pair with an anonymized output written as `{N}-out.txt`. `N` seeds at 10000 for an empty directory, otherwise `max_leading_digit_run + 1` across every filename whose name begins with digits. Files sharing the same `N` group all artifacts of one capture â€” e.g., `10000-in.clinical.txt`, `10000-in.clinical.html`, and `10000-out.txt` are three artifacts of clinical capture 10000; `10001-in.nonclinical.txt` is the lone artifact of non-clinical capture 10001. Gaps in the numeric sequence are not filled â€” the scan advances past the current maximum. Filenames that do not begin with a digit are ignored in the max calculation, so `apcap.log` and any user-placed `README` or `notes` co-exist without perturbing indexing. The scan parses the leading digit run, not the full stem, so legacy bare `{N}.{ext}` and prior untagged `{N}-in.{ext}` files from earlier prototype runs still count toward index advancement â€” all styles share one index space. **Flavors.** Every flavor the `arboard` abstraction exposes at the time of capture: | Flavor | Filename | Present when | |--------|----------|--------------| | Plain text (`get_text`) | `{N}-in.{tag}.txt` | Always â€” required for any focus handler entry | | HTML (`get().html()`) | `{N}-in.{tag}.html` | Epic "Copy All" places HTML; absence is possible and non-fatal | RTF and image flavors are documented gaps: `arboard` 3.x does not surface RTF, and image capture requires the `image-data` feature which this project does not enable. Dropping to platform-specific pasteboard APIs to close these gaps is explicitly out of scope for the prototype. **Anonymized output.** On every Clinical-branch focus, after the input harvest succeeds, the focus handler also writes `{N}-out.txt`: the anonymizer's output with default-elide toggle states (every finding elided). The point is a legible input/output pairing on disk â€” Brad can diff `{N}-in.clinical.txt` against `{N}-out.txt` to review what clipbuddy produced for Ann, without relying on her to paste-and-save. This output is captured *at focus time*, not on copy. Updating `{N}-out.txt` as Ann toggles findings or re-copies is deferred UX; the current snapshot is always the default-elide baseline. Write failure is non-fatal â€” triage continues, and the failure is logged via `apcrl_error_now!`. An HTML-preserving anonymized output (`{N}-out.html`) is out of scope â€” the current anonymizer is plain-text only. Non-clinical captures do not pair with an anonymized output; there is nothing to anonymize because no findings are produced. **Failure mode.** A capture failure logs via `apcrl_error_now!` (stdout + tee) and does not abort triage. User-visible behavior is unchanged whether harvest succeeds or fails â€” the triage pipeline is authoritative. The same holds for the anonymized-output write. System-clipboard zero-out happens only on the Clinical branch; a non-clinical capture leaves Ann's clipboard untouched. **Privacy posture.** PHI-at-rest stays outside the repo. The journal directory is PHI-permissive by design â€” harvests, the anonymized-output pairings, and the observability log all share the same on-device-only posture: nothing in `$HOME/apcjd/` is auto-committed, auto-uploaded, or auto-anonymized. Review and promotion of any artifact to an out-of-site context is a manual act. The always-harvest posture is a deliberate scope expansion over earlier prototype behavior, which only captured on the Clinical branch. Today's compact is "clipbuddy captures every clipboard it sees during a session" â€” not only clinical notes. When Brad reviews a session's journal directory he will observe Ann's non-clinical clipboard activity too: passwords she copied, personal email snippets, URLs, anything at all that hit her pasteboard while Ann's PHI Clipbuddy had focus in the background. This is not a new *kind* of risk â€” the journal directory was already PHI-permissive on-device, and nothing leaves the device automatically â€” but the *scope* of what a reviewer sees is broader, and naming that here means the posture is not discovered by surprise. A debug-mode or opt-out flag for non-clinical capture is deliberately not provided at prototype scale; revisit if Ann or Brad flags the scope as too broad in practice. ## Observability Log **Purpose.** Give the clinician-developer and the engineer a shared, persistent view of what the app did across runs. The `.app` bundle on macOS captures stdout where a non-technical user won't look for it; a file in the journal directory is where both humans already know to peek. **Mechanism.** `apcrl_log` exposes an optional file-tee sink: `apcrl_tee_init(path)` installs a once-only append-open handle. Every subsequent `apcrl_*_now!`, `_if!`, and comparison-variant emission is written twice â€” once to stdout (verbatim) and once to the tee file (same format, same line). One format, two sinks. **Format.** Exactly what stdout receives: `[LEVEL] [YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS.mmm] [file:line] message`. The timestamp is local wall-clock with millisecond precision, captured at emit time via `chrono::Local::now()`. Single emission path means no format drift between modalities â€” an engineer debugging a remote bundle and an engineer running `cargo run` read the same lines. The clock lets Ann's "it stopped working around 2pm" kind of report meet the log without re-running under a stopwatch. **Location.** `$HOME/apcjd/apcap.log`. Filename matches the binary name (`apcap`) as an engineer mnemonic. Append-only â€” no rotation at prototype scale; a clinical session produces tens of lines, a year of use produces thousands. **Init.** At `main()` startup, before any other log emission. Failure to install the tee (missing HOME, disk full, permissions) logs to stdout and proceeds â€” the app starts and stdout remains authoritative. **Failure mode.** Tee write failures are silently swallowed inside the emitter to avoid recursive-logging hazards (a failing log should not generate more log traffic). Stdout is the authoritative sink; tee is best-effort. **Privacy posture.** The log lives alongside harvests in the journal directory and inherits the same posture â€” the whole directory is PHI-permissive by design. The log may carry clipboard content, PHI-adjacent details, or anything else useful to diagnose behavior on the clinician's machine. The discipline that matters is not "keep the log PHI-free" but "keep the journal directory on the doctor's computer" â€” nothing in `$HOME/apcjd/` is auto-uploaded, auto-committed, or transmitted off-device. Review and promotion of any artifact (capture or log line) to an out-of-site context is a manual act. ## Detection Pipeline ### Overview Three tiers execute in sequence. Results merge by highest severity â€” if a token is flagged by multiple tiers, the most severe classification wins. ``` Input: Epic HTML clipboard content | Parse HTML â†’ typed spans with labels and positions | Tier 1: Regex scan â†’ RED | Tier 2: Label-anchored extraction â†’ RED | Tier 3: Dictionary blacklist/whitelist scan â†’ YELLOW or PASS | Merge by highest severity | Output: classified token stream â†’ frontend ``` ### Tier 1 â€” Regex Patterns (â†’ RED) Structural patterns with low false positive rate. No database dependency. **Known ambiguities:** The zip code regex (`\d{5}`) can match 5-digit lab values, identifiers, or dosage amounts. The date regex can match version numbers or other formatted numerics. The street address regex can match non-address text with number-word-suffix patterns. These are accepted risks for the prototype â€” the conservative bias (false positive > false negative) means these appear as RED items the clinician can review. Context-aware refinement is deferred. | Pattern | Category | Regex (representative) | Notes | |---------|----------|----------------------|-------| | SSN | `[SSN]` | `\d{3}-\d{2}-\d{4}` | | | Phone | `[PHONE]` | `\d{3}[-.)]\s?\d{3}[-.)]\s?\d{4}`, `\(\d{3}\)\s?\d{3}-\d{4}` | Multiple formats | | Email | `[EMAIL]` | `[\w.+-]+@[\w-]+\.[\w.-]+` | | | Dates | `[DATE]` | `\d{1,2}/\d{1,2}/\d{2,4}`, `\d{1,2}-\d{1,2}-\d{2,4}`, month name patterns | Multiple formats | | Street address | `[ADDRESS]` | `\d+\s+[A-Z]\w+\s+(St\|Dr\|Ave\|Blvd\|Rd\|Ln\|Way\|Ct\|Pl\|Cir\|Drive\|Street\|Avenue\|Boulevard\|Road\|Lane\|Court\|Place\|Circle)` | Followed by optional city/state/zip | | Zip code | `[ADDRESS]` | `\b\d{5}(-\d{4})?\b` | Within address context | | MRN (labeled) | `[MRN]` | Value following `MRN:` label | | | Account (labeled) | `[ACCOUNT]` | Value following `Account` or `Acct` label | | | Encounter ID (labeled) | `[ENCOUNTER_ID]` | Value following `Encounter ID:` label | | Dates receive special handling: when a date follows a `DOB:` label, the engine computes age from the current date and emits `Age: NN` instead of `[DATE]` in the anonymized output. Per HIPAA Safe Harbor (45 CFR 164.514(b)(2)(i)(C)), ages over 89 are aggregated: computed age >89 emits `Age: 90+`. ### Tier 2 â€” Label-Anchored Extraction (â†’ RED) Words immediately following known Epic labels are PHI regardless of dictionary membership. The label is authoritative â€” if Epic says `Patient: Xylophone McZephyr`, that's a name even if no database contains it. | Label Pattern | Category | Extraction Rule | |--------------|----------|-----------------| | `Patient:` | `[NAME]` | All text until next label or line break | | `Attending:` | `[PROVIDER]` | All text until next label or line break | | `Provider:` | `[PROVIDER]` | All text until next label or line break | | `Referring:` | `[PROVIDER]` | All text until next label or line break | | `Facility:` | `[FACILITY]` | All text until next label or line break | | `Electronically signed by:` | `[PROVIDER]` | All text until next label or line break | | `Emergency contact` | `[NAME]` | Name portion, phone extracted by Tier 1 | | `primary care physician` | `[PROVIDER]` | Following name (e.g., "Dr. Susan Chen") | Label matching is case-insensitive on the label text. Value extraction extends to the next structural boundary (label, line break, or HTML element boundary). **Per-word anchoring:** Each word in the extracted value is individually flagged as RED. "Margaret J. Thornton" after `Patient:` produces three separate **anchored** items, each classified `[NAME]`. In anonymized output: `[NAME] [NAME] [NAME]`. No multi-word merge is attempted. Items caught by Tier 2 are **anchored** â€” their RED classification is final. They are not subsequently dictionary-checked. If a word is both anchored (Tier 2) and matched (Tier 3), the anchored classification takes precedence. ### Vocabulary: Anchored vs. Matched Two independent mechanisms flag words as potential PHI. To avoid confusion, the spec distinguishes: - **Anchored**: Flagged by Tier 2 because of *position* â€” the word follows a known label. Always RED. Independent of any dictionary. - **Matched**: Flagged by Tier 3 because of *identity* â€” the word appears in a name/location dictionary. YELLOW (unless also anchored, in which case RED wins). A word can be both anchored and matched (e.g., "Thornton" after `Patient:` AND in the Census surname list). Anchored always takes precedence. The term "blacklist" in this spec refers specifically to the Tier 3 name/location dictionaries; Tier 2 label-anchored extraction is a separate mechanism. ### Tier 3 â€” Dictionary Blacklist/Whitelist (â†’ YELLOW or PASS) Catches names and locations in narrative text that lack label anchors (e.g., "her husband Robert Thornton called 911"). **Case handling:** All dictionary lookups are case-insensitive. The aho-corasick automaton is built from lowercase dictionary entries. Input tokens are lowercased before matching. Original case is preserved in display and anonymized output. **Process:** 1. Segment text into words using `unicode-segmentation` (UAX#29 boundaries) 2. Lowercase each token for lookup; preserve original for display 3. Run aho-corasick automaton against lowercased tokens (single pass, O(n)) 4. For each hit, check whitelist membership (also lowercase) 5. Classify: - Blacklist hit, no whitelist hit â†’ **YELLOW** (matched, default ELIDE) - Blacklist hit AND whitelist hit â†’ **YELLOW** (collision, default ELIDE) - Whitelist hit only â†’ **PASS** - Neither list â†’ **PASS** **Collision resolution context signals** (applied within YELLOW to aid clinician review): - Capitalization mid-sentence suggests a proper noun (name) rather than common word - Adjacent blacklist hits suggest a full name ("Robert Thornton" â€” two hits together) - Section context: names in demographics header vs. narrative HPI carry different weight These signals inform the findings panel display but do not change the YELLOW classification in the prototype. The clinician makes the final call. ## Dictionary Data ### Proof-of-Life Sizing Curated-small dictionaries that prove the architecture without requiring full datasets: | Dictionary | Source | License | Size | File | |------------|--------|---------|------|------| | Surnames | US Census 2010, top 1,000 by frequency | Public domain | ~1,000 entries | `dictionaries/surnames.txt` | | First names | SSA baby names, top 1,000 | Public domain | ~1,000 entries | `dictionaries/firstnames.txt` | | Cities | US state capitals + top 100 cities by population | Public domain | ~150 entries | `dictionaries/cities.txt` | | Medical whitelist | Hand-curated: common medications, lab tests, diagnoses, anatomy, abbreviations | Original curation | ~500 entries | `dictionaries/medical_whitelist.txt` | | English whitelist | Top 5,000 common English words | Public domain | ~5,000 entries | `dictionaries/english_whitelist.txt` | Total: ~8,000 entries, ~80KB on disk. ### Full Dataset Sizing (Future) | Dictionary | Source | License | Size | |------------|--------|---------|------| | Surnames | US Census 2010, complete | Public domain | ~160,000 | | First names | SSA baby names, complete | Public domain | ~100,000 | | Cities | US Census incorporated places | Public domain | ~30,000 | | Medical whitelist | SNOMED-CT + RxNorm + LOINC subsets | NLM license (redistribution constraints) | ~100,000+ | | English whitelist | Full dictionary | Public domain | ~170,000 | ## Anonymization ### Placeholder Strategy Category-specific placeholders preserve semantic context for Open Evidence: | PHI Category | Placeholder | Example | |-------------|-------------|---------| | Patient/person names | `[NAME]` | "Ms. [NAME] is a 74-year-old female" | | Dates (non-DOB) | `[DATE]` | "Visit Date: [DATE]" | | Date of birth | `Age: NN` or `Age: 90+` | "DOB: 03/15/1952" â†’ "Age: 74"; ages >89 â†’ "Age: 90+" | | Medical record number | `[MRN]` | "MRN: [MRN]" | | Phone numbers | `[PHONE]` | "phone: [PHONE]" | | Addresses | `[ADDRESS]` | "at [ADDRESS]" | | Email addresses | `[EMAIL]` | | | Social Security numbers | `[SSN]` | | | Facility names | `[FACILITY]` | "Seen at [FACILITY]" | | Provider names | `[PROVIDER]` | "Dr. [PROVIDER]" | | Account numbers | `[ACCOUNT]` | | | Encounter IDs | `[ENCOUNTER_ID]` | | ### Output Format Plain text with preserved section structure (line breaks, section headers). HTML formatting is stripped â€” this is itself a privacy measure, removing embedded metadata and hidden fields. The output should be readable when pasted into Open Evidence's chat input. ## Self-Update Mechanism The application watches `/Users/Shared/apcua/` for new `.app` bundles using the `notify` crate (FSEvents backend on macOS). On detection: 1. Copy new bundle over current application path 2. Spawn `open -n ` to launch new version 3. `std::process::exit(0)` to terminate current process No confirmation dialog. The watcher runs on a background thread independent of the main Tauri event loop. If the watch directory does not exist at startup, the app creates it silently. ## Project Structure ``` Tools/apck/ apck-claude-context.md # Build/run/deploy instructions (RCG reference) APCAS-Specification.md # Application spec (UX vision) APCPS-PrototypeSpecification.md # This document (pipeline engineering) apcw_workbench.sh # Bash workbench apcz_zipper.sh # Zipper enrollment test_fixtures/ epic_progress_note.html # Synthetic Epic clipboard data apcd/ # Rust/Tauri source directory Cargo.toml # Two [[bin]]: apcap (app), apcal (fixture loader) src/ apcap_main.rs # Tauri app entry point apcal_main.rs # Fixture loader entry point lib.rs # Engine library + test module declarations apcre_engine.rs # PHI detection orchestrator apcte_engine.rs # Tests for engine apcrp_parse.rs # HTML clipboard parsing apctp_parse.rs # Tests for parser apcrm_match.rs # Dictionary/regex matching apctm_match.rs # Tests for matching apcrd_dictionaries.rs # Dictionary loading apctd_dictionaries.rs # Tests for dictionaries apcru_update.rs # Directory watcher + self-update (no unit tests) ui/ index.html style.css dictionaries/ surnames.txt firstnames.txt cities.txt medical_whitelist.txt english_whitelist.txt ``` ## Prefix Tree ``` apc (non-terminal) â”œâ”€â”€ apca (non-terminal) â”‚ â”œâ”€â”€ apcab â€” App Batch binary (assay â€” detection pipeline on HTML files) â”‚ â”œâ”€â”€ apcad â€” App Dictionary binary (refresh from public sources) â”‚ â”œâ”€â”€ apcal â€” App Loader binary (fixture clipboard tool) â”‚ â”œâ”€â”€ apcap â€” App Prototype binary (Tauri main) â”‚ â””â”€â”€ apcas â€” application specification document (UX, workflow) â”œâ”€â”€ apcc â€” CLI command implementations â”œâ”€â”€ apcd â€” Rust/Tauri source directory â”‚ â”œâ”€â”€ apcrh â€” Clipboard harvest module (writes to journal directory) â”‚ â”œâ”€â”€ apcrj â€” Journal directory path resolver â”‚ â””â”€â”€ apcrl â€” Logging macros (info, error, fatal with file/line) + file-tee sink â”œâ”€â”€ apcj (non-terminal â€” journal) â”‚ â””â”€â”€ apcjd â€” journal directory ($HOME/apcjd/) holding harvests + apcap.log â”œâ”€â”€ apck â€” kit directory â”œâ”€â”€ apcps â€” prototype specification document â”œâ”€â”€ apcs (non-terminal) â”‚ â””â”€â”€ apcs0 â€” detection pipeline specification (MCM concept model) â”œâ”€â”€ apcu (non-terminal) â”‚ â””â”€â”€ apcua â€” update staging directory (/Users/Shared/apcua/) â”œâ”€â”€ apcw â€” workbench â””â”€â”€ apcz â€” zipper ``` Rust source file prefixes follow RCG: `{cipher}r{classifier}_{name}.rs` where cipher is `apc`. Test files: `{cipher}t{classifier}_{name}.rs` â€” classifier matches between source and test. See RCG (Rust Coding Guide) for full naming conventions, including `z`-prefix internal declarations, string boundary discipline, and constant discipline. `lib.rs` wiring: ```rust pub mod apcre_engine; pub mod apcrp_parse; pub mod apcrm_match; pub mod apcrd_dictionaries; pub mod apcru_update; pub mod apcrh_harvest; pub mod apcrj_journal; #[cfg(test)] mod apcte_engine; #[cfg(test)] mod apctp_parse; #[cfg(test)] mod apctm_match; #[cfg(test)] mod apctd_dictionaries; #[cfg(test)] mod apcth_harvest; ``` ## Tabtargets | Tabtarget | Colophon | Purpose | |-----------|----------|---------| | `tt/apcw-b.Build.sh` | `apcw-b` | `cargo tauri build` (release) | | `tt/apcw-r.Run.sh` | `apcw-r` | `cargo tauri dev` (local development) | | `tt/apcw-D.Deploy.sh` | `apcw-D` | Build + scp to `anns-macbook-air:/Users/Shared/apcua/` | | `tt/apcw-fl.FixtureLoad.sh` | `apcw-fl` | Run `apcal` to load fixture HTML onto clipboard | | `tt/apcw-t.Test.sh` | `apcw-t` | `cargo test` in `apcd/` |

1	## Ann's PHI Clipbuddy Kit (APCK)
2	
3	APCK is a Tauri desktop app that intercepts clipboard content from Epic EHR, detects PHI via a three-tier engine (regex, label-anchored, dictionary), presents a red/yellow/grey triage view, and writes anonymized plain text to clipboard for pasting into Open Evidence.
